definitions.definition: Biomolecules are organic molecules found in living organisms. They are the building blocks of life and include carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids.
Polysaccharides are complex carbohydrates formed by the condensation of many monosaccharide units.
Disaccharides are sugars formed by the condensation of two monosaccharide units.
Monosaccharides are simple sugars that cannot be hydrolysed further into simpler sugars.
Starch is a polysaccharide composed of amylose and amylopectin, stored in plants as energy reserve.
Glycogen is a polysaccharide stored in animals as energy reserve.
Cellulose is a polysaccharide found in plant cell walls, providing structural support.
Proteins are biomolecules composed of amino acids, performing various functions in living organisms.
Amino acids are the building blocks of proteins, containing an amino group (-NH2) and a carboxyl group (-COOH).
Peptide bonds are covalent bonds formed between amino acids during protein synthesis.
Enzymes are proteins that act as biological catalysts, speeding up chemical reactions in living organisms.
Nucleic acids are biomolecules composed of nucleotides, responsible for storing and transmitting genetic information.
DNA is a type of nucleic acid that contains the genetic instructions for an organism's development and function.
RNA is a type of nucleic acid that plays a crucial role in protein synthesis and other cellular processes.
Nucleotides are the building blocks of nucleic acids, composed of a nitrogenous base, a sugar molecule, and a phosphate group.
Phosphodiester bonds are covalent bonds formed between nucleotides during nucleic acid synthesis.
Lipids are biomolecules that include fats, oils, and waxes, serving as energy storage and structural components in living organisms.
Glycerol is a polyol found in triglycerides, providing a backbone for fatty acid chains.
